title: Count Palindromic Subsequences of Length 5
description: Count palindromic subsequences of fixed length 5

examples.explanation: String length n=5. We need all combinations of 5 indices from 0-4. Only one combination: i=0,j=1,k=2,l=3,m=4. Check: s[0]='a', s[4]='a' match. s[1]='b', s[3]='b' match. Valid palindrome. Count=1.

n=5. Only combination: indices 0,1,2,3,4. s[0]='a', s[4]='a' match. s[1]='a', s[3]='a' match. Valid. Count=1. Note: Even though all characters are same, there's only one subsequence of length 5 (the whole string).

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n⁵)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible subsequences of length 5 by iterating through all combinations of 5 indices in the string, check each if it forms a palindrome by comparing characters, and count the valid ones. Use nested loops to explore all index choices.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Count of Palindromic Subsequences of Length 5
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using nested loops to generate all 5-index combinations and check for palindromes.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to count palindromic subsequences of exactly length 5. The intuition is straightforward: since we need subsequences of fixed length 5, we can generate all possible combinations of 5 indices from the string, extract the corresponding characters, and check if they form a palindrome. A palindrome of length 5 has the pattern a-b-c-b-a, meaning the first and fifth characters must match, and the second and fourth characters must match, with the middle character being any character. We use five nested loops to iterate through all possible index combinations where i<j<k<l<m to ensure we're selecting indices in increasing order (maintaining subsequence order). For each combination, we check if s[i]==s[m] and s[j]==s[l]. If both conditions are true, we have a valid palindromic subsequence and increment our count. The algorithm: 1. Initialize count=0. 2. For i from 0 to n-5: For j from i+1 to n-4: For k from j+1 to n-3: For l from k+1 to n-2: For m from l+1 to n-1: if s[i]==s[m] and s[j]==s[l]: count++. 3. Return count. Time complexity is O(n⁵) because we have five nested loops each iterating up to n times in worst case. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include: not maintaining strict index ordering (i<j<k<l<m), which would count invalid subsequences; off-by-one errors in loop bounds; and misunderstanding that subsequences don't need to be contiguous. The brute force approach is conceptually simple but computationally expensive, making it impractical for large n but excellent for understanding the problem fundamentals.

examples.trace: String length n=5. We need all combinations of 5 indices from 0-4. Only one combination: i=0,j=1,k=2,l=3,m=4. Check: s[0]='a', s[4]='a' match. s[1]='b', s[3]='b' match. Valid palindrome. Count=1.

n=5. Only combination: indices 0,1,2,3,4. s[0]='a', s[4]='a' match. s[1]='a', s[3]='a' match. Valid. Count=1. Note: Even though all characters are same, there's only one subsequence of length 5 (the whole string).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate and Check All 5-Index Combinations
ladders.desc: The nested loop structure that generates all valid index combinations and checks palindrome conditions.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to count subsequences of length 5, we need to examine every possible way to choose 5 positions from the string while maintaining their original order. We implement this using five nested loops with carefully chosen bounds to ensure i<j<k<l<m. For each combination, we check the palindrome condition: the first and last characters must be equal, and the second and fourth characters must be equal (the middle character can be anything). The algorithm: 1. Initialize count=0, n=len(s). 2. Loop i from 0 to n-5 (ensures room for 4 more indices). 3. Loop j from i+1 to n-4. 4. Loop k from j+1 to n-3. 5. Loop l from k+1 to n-2. 6. Loop m from l+1 to n-1. 7. Inside innermost loop: if s[i]==s[m] and s[j]==s[l]: count++. 8. Return count. Time complexity is O(n⁵) due to five nested loops. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the complete counting mechanism. Common mistakes: incorrect loop bounds leading to out-of-range errors; not maintaining strict inequality between indices; forgetting that subsequences preserve order. The pattern ensures we systematically enumerate all C(n,5) combinations without repetition.

examples.trace: n=3. Need 5 indices but only 3 available. All loops will have zero iterations because n-5=-2, etc. No combinations possible. Count=0.
n=4. Need 5 indices but only 4 available. Similar to above, loops won't execute. Count=0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Palindrome Check for 5-Character Sequences
ladders.desc: The condition that determines if a 5-character sequence forms a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the palindrome verification logic for sequences of exactly 5 characters. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. For length 5, this means positions 1 and 5 must match, positions 2 and 4 must match, and position 3 (the middle) can be any character. Formally, given characters c1,c2,c3,c4,c5, we need c1==c5 AND c2==c4. The middle character c3 doesn't affect the palindrome property. This is simpler than checking all pairs because of the fixed length. The algorithm: 1. Receive 5 characters as input. 2. Return true if char1==char5 and char2==char4, else false. Time O(1), space O(1). This connects to L1 as the condition checked inside the innermost loop. Common mistakes: checking all pairs unnecessarily (c1==c5, c2==c4, c3==c3); misunderstanding that the middle character must match itself; or incorrectly requiring c1==c4 and c2==c5. The simplicity of this check is key to the brute force efficiency.

examples.trace: Check: c1='a', c5='a' match. c2='b', c4='b' match. Both conditions true, so palindrome.
Check: c1='a', c5='a' match. c2='b', c4='d' do not match. Second condition false, so not palindrome.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generating All Combinations of 5 Indices from a String
ladders.desc: Creating all possible sets of 5 indices in increasing order from 0 to n-1.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves generating all ways to choose 5 positions from n available indices, ensuring the chosen indices are in strictly increasing order to maintain subsequence order. The intuition is combinatorial: we need to enumerate all 5-element subsets of {0,1,...,n-1}. For brute force, we use nested loops where each loop variable represents one of the five indices, with each inner loop starting after the previous index to maintain order. The algorithm: 1. Let n be string length. 2. For i from 0 to n-5: For j from i+1 to n-4: For k from j+1 to n-3: For l from k+1 to n-2: For m from l+1 to n-1: process combination (i,j,k,l,m). The loop bounds ensure we always have enough remaining indices for subsequent loops. Time complexity is O(n⁵) in worst case. Space O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the index combinations that yield the 5-character sequences to check. Common mistakes: using ≤ instead of < in loop conditions; starting inner loops from 0 instead of previous index+1; incorrect bounds causing out-of-range errors. This systematic generation is fundamental to exploring all possibilities.

examples.trace: Only one combination possible: i=0 (0 to 0), j=1 (1 to 1), k=2 (2 to 2), l=3 (3 to 3), m=4 (4 to 4). Result: [(0,1,2,3,4)].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Characters by Index in a String
ladders.desc: Retrieving the character at a specific position in a string using index notation.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing individual characters in a string using their positional indices. The intuition is that a string is a sequence of characters where each character has a position starting from 0. For example, in string s="abc", s[0]='a', s[1]='b', s[2]='c'. This is crucial for our problem because we need to compare characters at specific indices to check palindrome conditions. The algorithm is simple: given string s and index i, return s[i]. Time O(1), space O(1). This connects to L3 as we use this operation to get characters from the generated index combinations. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing (s[1] for first character); accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., s[3] for "abc"); confusing strings with lists in languages where strings are immutable. Proper indexing is essential for correct character comparison.

examples.trace: String indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. Access s[1] returns 'e'.
s[0] is first character 'p'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understanding that a subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some characters without changing order.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: a subsequence of a string is any sequence that can be obtained by deleting zero or more characters from the original string, while maintaining the relative order of the remaining characters. Unlike substrings, subsequences do not need to be contiguous. For example, for string "abc", subsequences include "", "a", "b", "c", "ab", "ac", "bc", "abc". The intuition is that we're selecting a subset of positions from the original string in order. This is crucial for our problem because we're counting palindromic subsequences of length 5, meaning we choose 5 positions from the string (in increasing order) and check if those characters form a palindrome. Key properties: order is preserved; characters are not rearranged; not necessarily contiguous. This connects to L4 as we'll access characters at chosen indices. Common mistakes: confusing subsequences with substrings (which are contiguous); thinking subsequences can reorder characters; or including empty sequence when not needed. Understanding this definition is essential before tackling any subsequence problem.

examples.trace: Start with empty: ''. Choose indices: {0} gives 'a', {1} gives 'b', {2} gives 'c', {0,1} gives 'ab', {0,2} gives 'ac', {1,2} gives 'bc', {0,1,2} gives 'abc'. All maintain order.
Choose indices 0 and 2: characters 'a' and 'a', order preserved. This is a subsequence even though not contiguous.

approaches.name: Prefix and Suffix Combination O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each possible middle character in the palindrome (the third character of length 5), precompute prefix counts of character pairs before it and suffix counts after it, then combine these to count valid subsequences where the outer two pairs match. Use arrays to store frequencies for efficient lookup.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Palindromic Subsequences of Length 5 Using Prefix-Suffix Combination
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution counting all length-5 palindromic subsequences by combining precomputed prefix and suffix character pair frequencies.
ladders.explanation:
This solution leverages the fixed length of 5 to break the palindrome into three parts: the first two characters (outer left pair), the middle character (third position), and the last two characters (outer right pair). For a valid palindrome of length 5, the first and fifth characters must match, the second and fourth must match, and the middle character can be any character. The key insight is to treat the middle character as a pivot. For each possible middle position 'mid' (index from 2 to n-3, since we need at least 2 characters before and after), we precompute all possible pairs of characters that could appear before 'mid' (prefix pairs) and after 'mid' (suffix pairs). Specifically, for each character pair (a,b) where a and b are any of the 26 lowercase letters, we count how many times the pair (a,b) appears as a subsequence in the prefix (indices < mid) and similarly in the suffix (indices > mid). Then, for each middle character at position 'mid', we iterate over all possible character pairs (a,b). The number of palindromic subsequences with this middle character and outer pair (a,b) is: (count of (a,b) as subsequence in prefix) * (count of (b,a) as subsequence in suffix). We multiply because for each occurrence of (a,b) before mid and (b,a) after mid, we can combine them with the middle character to form a valid palindrome (a,b,mid_char,b,a). Summing over all middle positions and all character pairs gives the total count.

Intuition: By fixing the middle, we reduce the problem to counting matching pairs on left and right, which is efficient because we only need to track frequencies of character pairs (26*26=676 possibilities) rather than all subsequences. The O(n²) time comes from: for each middle position (O(n)), we need to compute prefix and suffix pair counts. We can compute these counts dynamically: as we move the middle from left to right, we update prefix counts by including characters before the current middle, and suffix counts by excluding characters that are no longer after the middle. However, a simpler implementation precomputes for each middle by scanning left and right, which is O(n²) overall.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize total_count = 0.
2. For each index mid from 2 to n-3 (inclusive):
   a. Compute prefix_counts[26][26] = 0. For all i from 0 to mid-1 and j from i+1 to mid-1, increment prefix_counts[s[i]][s[j]] by 1. This counts all ordered pairs (a,b) in the prefix.
   b. Compute suffix_counts[26][26] = 0. For all i from mid+1 to n-1 and j from i+1 to n-1, increment suffix_counts[s[i]][s[j]] by 1. This counts all ordered pairs (a,b) in the suffix.
   c. For each character pair (a,b) where a and b are from 'a' to 'z':
      total_count += prefix_counts[a][b] * suffix_counts[b][a]
3. Return total_count.

Time complexity: O(n²) because for each of O(n) middle positions, we compute prefix and suffix counts by nested loops over O(n) indices each, and the pair iteration is constant (676). Space: O(1) for the count arrays (676 integers), but O(n) for the input string.

Connection to L1: This uses the helper function from L1 to compute pair counts efficiently, but here we integrate it into the full loop.

Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices (e.g., not ensuring at least 2 characters before/after middle), forgetting that pairs are ordered (a,b) vs (b,a), or not using modulo arithmetic if counts are large (use 10^9+7). Also, inefficient recomputation of counts for each middle can be optimized by dynamic updates.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def count_palindromic_subseq_length5(s):
    n = len(s)
    if n < 5: return 0
    total = 0
    MOD = 10**9+7
    for mid in range(2, n-2):
        pref = [[0]*26 for _ in range(26)]
        for i in range(mid):
            for j in range(i+1, mid):
                a = ord(s[i]) - ord('a')
                b = ord(s[j]) - ord('a')
                pref[a][b] += 1
        suff = [[0]*26 for _ in range(26)]
        for i in range(mid+1, n):
            for j in range(i+1, n):
                a = ord(s[i]) - ord('a')
                b = ord(s[j]) - ord('a')
                suff[a][b] += 1
        for a in range(26):
            for b in range(26):
                total = (total + pref[a][b] * suff[b][a]) % MOD
    return total

examples.trace: String length n=5. Only middle position possible: mid=2 (0-indexed, character 'a'). Prefix indices: 0,1 (characters 'a','b'). Compute prefix_counts: pairs: (0,1): ('a','b') count=1. Suffix indices: 3,4 (characters 'b','a'): pairs: (3,4): ('b','a') count=1. Iterate pairs: For (a,b)=('a','b'): pref['a']['b']=1, suff['b']['a']=1, product=1. Total=1. Other pairs yield 0. So output=1. The palindrome is "ababa" itself.

n=5, all 'a'. Only mid=2. Prefix indices 0,1: pairs: (0,1): ('a','a') count=1. Suffix indices 3,4: pairs: (3,4): ('a','a') count=1. For (a,b)=('a','a'): pref=1, suff['a']['a']=1, product=1. Total=1. Only one distinct subsequence "aaaaa".

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute All Ordered Character Pair Counts in a Substring
ladders.desc: Given a string, count frequencies of all ordered pairs (a,b) where a and b are characters and a appears before b as a subsequence.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core of the full solution. For a given substring (or entire string), we need to compute how many times each ordered pair of characters (a,b) appears as a subsequence where character 'a' occurs at some index i and character 'b' occurs at a later index j (i < j). This is essential because in the palindrome problem, the prefix and suffix require counting such pairs to match outer characters. The intuition is to iterate over all possible pairs of indices (i,j) with i < j, and increment the count for the pair (s[i], s[j]). Since there are only 26 lowercase letters, we can use a 26x26 array to store counts.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize a 2D array counts[26][26] with zeros.
2. Let n be the length of the input string s.
3. For i from 0 to n-2:
   a. For j from i+1 to n-1:
      i. Let a = s[i], b = s[j].
      ii. Convert a and b to indices (0-25) by subtracting 'a'.
      iii. Increment counts[a][b] by 1.
4. Return the counts array.

Time complexity: O(m²) where m is the length of the substring, because of nested loops over all pairs. Space: O(1) for the array (676 integers).

Connection to L0: In the full problem, this sub-routine is called twice for each middle position: once for the prefix (indices before mid) and once for the suffix (indices after mid). The results are then combined.

Common mistakes: Confusing ordered vs unordered pairs (here ordered matters because (a,b) and (b,a) are different), or not handling empty substrings correctly (should return all zeros). Also, ensure indices are within bounds.

Real code pattern:
def count_ordered_pairs(s):
    n = len(s)
    counts = [[0]*26 for _ in range(26)]
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            a = ord(s[i]) - ord('a')
            b = ord(s[j]) - ord('a')
            counts[a][b] += 1
    return counts

examples.trace: n=2. i=0, j=1: s[0]='a' (index 0), s[1]='b' (index 1). Increment counts[0][1] by 1. No other pairs. So counts[0][1]=1.

n=3. Pairs: (0,1): ('a','b') from indices (0,1) and (0,2) since s[2]='a' but ordered pair is ('a','b') only if second is 'b'? Wait: indices: i=0,j=1: ('a','b') counts[0][1]+=1. i=0,j=2: ('a','a') counts[0][0]+=1. i=1,j=2: ('b','a') counts[1][0]+=1. So counts[0][1]=1, counts[0][0]=1, counts[1][0]=1. Correction: Actually for i=0,j=2, s[2]='a', so pair ('a','a') yes. So output as above.

testCases.expected: counts[0][1]=1, counts[0][2]=1, counts[0][0]=1, counts[1][2]=1, counts[1][0]=1, counts[2][0]=1
counts based on letters (non-lowercase, but assume lowercase): e.g., 'h','e','l','l','o'

ladders.title: Core Logic — Matching Prefix and Suffix Pairs for a Fixed Middle
ladders.desc: Given counts of ordered pairs in prefix and suffix, compute number of valid length-5 palindromic subsequences for a fixed middle character.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the central combinatorial insight: once we have the counts of all ordered pairs in the prefix (characters before the middle) and suffix (characters after the middle), we can compute how many palindromic subsequences have this specific middle character. For a palindrome of length 5 with middle character c, the sequence is of the form a,b,c,b,a where a and b are characters. This means the outer pairs must be symmetric: the first two characters are (a,b) and the last two are (b,a). Therefore, for each possible pair (a,b), if there are X occurrences of (a,b) as a subsequence in the prefix and Y occurrences of (b,a) as a subsequence in the suffix, then the number of palindromes with middle c and outer pair (a,b) is X * Y. We sum this over all a,b from 'a' to 'z'.

Intuition: The prefix provides choices for the first two characters, and the suffix provides choices for the last two characters. Since the palindrome requires the last two to be the reverse of the first two, we match prefix pairs with the reversed suffix pairs. Multiplication counts all combinations.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: prefix_counts[26][26], suffix_counts[26][26] (from L1).
2. Initialize total = 0.
3. For a from 0 to 25:
   a. For b from 0 to 25:
      i. total += prefix_counts[a][b] * suffix_counts[b][a]
4. Return total.

Time complexity: O(1) since 26*26=676 iterations, constant time. Space: O(1).

Connection to L1: This uses the pair counts computed by L1's sub-routine.

Common mistakes: Swapping a and b incorrectly (must be prefix[a][b] and suffix[b][a]), or not considering all pairs (including when a=b). Also, integer overflow if counts are large—use modulo.

Real code pattern:
def count_for_middle(prefix_counts, suffix_counts):
    total = 0
    for a in range(26):
        for b in range(26):
            total += prefix_counts[a][b] * suffix_counts[b][a]
    return total

examples.trace: Let a=0,b=1. prefix_counts[0][1]=1, suffix_counts[1][0]=1. Product=1. Sum over all a,b: only this pair contributes, so total=1.
Compute: For (a,a): prefix[0][0]=2, suffix[0][0]=1, product=2. For (a,b): prefix[0][1]=1, suffix[1][0]=2, product=2. Sum=4. Other pairs yield 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over All Pairs of Indices in a String
ladders.desc: Generate all ordered pairs of indices (i,j) with i < j in a given string, and process the corresponding characters.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept is fundamental to counting ordered pairs. Given a string of length n, we need to consider every possible pair of positions where the first index is less than the second. This is a common pattern in algorithms that involve subsequences or combinations of elements. The intuition is that to count pairs of characters that appear in order, we must examine all ways to choose two distinct positions from the string. This is essentially generating all 2-element combinations of indices, which number n*(n-1)/2.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: string s of length n.
2. For i from 0 to n-2:
   a. For j from i+1 to n-1:
      i. Access s[i] and s[j] as the characters at these indices.
      ii. Perform some operation (e.g., count, compare, etc.).

Time complexity: O(n²) due to nested loops. Space: O(1) aside from input.

Connection to L2: This is how we compute the prefix and suffix pair counts in L1—by iterating over all index pairs in the prefix and suffix substrings.

Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., using i <= n-2 or j <= n-1), or confusing i and j order. Also, not handling empty or single-character strings correctly (loops won't execute).

Real code pattern:
def iterate_pairs(s):
    n = len(s)
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            char1 = s[i]
            char2 = s[j]
            # do something with char1, char2

examples.trace: n=2. i=0: j=1 gives (0,1): 'a','a'. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Comparing Characters in a String by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve characters from specific positions in a string and compare them for equality or order.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational operation involves working with strings as sequences of characters. In many algorithms, including this palindrome problem, we need to access individual characters by their index (position) and perform operations like comparison. A string is typically zero-indexed, meaning the first character is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. Accessing a character at index i is usually an O(1) operation. Comparing two characters means checking if they are the same (for equality) or which comes first in alphabetical order (for ordering). In this problem, we compare characters to identify matching pairs for palindromes.

Intuition: Strings are immutable sequences, so we can think of them as arrays of characters. By accessing indices, we can examine the structure of the string, which is essential for counting subsequences.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Given a string s and indices i and j (ensure 0 <= i, j < len(s)).
2. Retrieve char1 = s[i] and char2 = s[j].
3. Compare: if char1 == char2, they match; otherwise, they are different.
4. Optionally, convert to integer indices (0-25 for lowercase letters) by subtracting 'a'.

Time complexity: O(1) per access/comparison. Space: O(1).

Connection to L3: This operation is used within the pair iteration to get the characters at each pair of indices.

Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (e.g., accessing index equal to length), forgetting that indices start at 0, or not handling case sensitivity (if applicable).

Real code pattern:
def access_and_compare(s, i, j):
    if i < 0 or i >= len(s) or j < 0 or j >= len(s):
        return None  # error
    char1 = s[i]
    char2 = s[j]
    are_equal = (char1 == char2)
    # convert to 0-25 if lowercase
    idx1 = ord(char1) - ord('a')
    idx2 = ord(char2) - ord('a')
    return are_equal, idx1, idx2

examples.trace: s[1] is the second character: 'e'. s[4] is the fifth character: 'o'. Compare: 'e' != 'o', so False.
s[0]='a', s[4]='a'. They are equal, so True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understand that a subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some characters without changing the order of the remaining characters.
ladders.explanation:
A subsequence is a fundamental concept in computer science and combinatorics. Given a string (or any sequence), a subsequence is formed by selecting some of its characters while maintaining their original relative order. Unlike a substring, which must be contiguous, a subsequence can skip characters. For example, in the string "abc", some subsequences are "a", "b", "c", "ab", "ac", "bc", "abc", and the empty string. The key property is that the order of characters in the subsequence must match the order in the original string. This is crucial for the palindrome problem because we are counting palindromic subsequences, not substrings—meaning the characters do not need to be adjacent in the original string.

Intuition: Think of the original string as a list of events. A subsequence is any selection of these events in the same sequence they occurred. This allows for flexibility, as we can ignore some characters to form desired patterns, like palindromes.

Step-by-step to identify if a sequence is a subsequence:
1. Let the original string be s of length n, and the candidate subsequence be t of length m.
2. Initialize pointers i=0 for s and j=0 for t.
3. While i < n and j < m:
   a. If s[i] == t[j], increment j (match found).
   b. Always increment i.
4. If j == m, then t is a subsequence of s; otherwise, it is not.

Time complexity for checking: O(n). Space: O(1).

Connection to L4: When we access characters by index in L4, we are often doing so to build or check subsequences.

Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring (which requires continuity), or not considering the empty subsequence. Also, overcounting when generating subsequences—order matters.

Real code pattern:
def is_subsequence(t, s):
    i, j = 0, 0
    while i < len(s) and j < len(t):
        if s[i] == t[j]:
            j += 1
        i += 1
    return j == len(t)

examples.trace: s: 'a','b','c'; t: 'a','c'. Start i=0,j=0: s[0]='a' matches t[0]='a', j=1. i=1: s[1]='b' no match. i=2: s[2]='c' matches t[1]='c', j=2. j==len(t), so True.
s: 'a','b','c'; t: 'c','a'. i=0,j=0: 'a' vs 'c' no match. i=1: 'b' vs 'c' no. i=2: 'c' vs 'c' match, j=1. End of s, j=1 not equal to len(t)=2, so False.
